title: Substring Distribution
description:
You are given a string s of length n (1 <= n <= 100000) consisting of lowercase letters a–z. For each length L from 1 to n, output how many distinct substrings of s have length exactly L.

Print n integers separated by spaces.

examples.explanation: Length 1: {a, b} -> 2; Length 2: {ab, ba} -> 2; Length 3: {aba, bab} -> 2; Length 4: {abab} -> 1.

mcqChain.questionText: For s = "abab", how many distinct substrings of length 1 are there?
mcqChain.diagram: Substrings length 1: a, b, a, b
mcqChain.explanation: Distinct letters are {a, b}, so 2. Counting positions would give 4 but distinct substrings count unique strings only.

wrongExplanations.3: There are 4 positions but only 2 distinct letters.

mcqChain.hint: List unique letters only.

mcqChain.questionText: For s = "abab", how many distinct substrings of length 2 are there?
mcqChain.explanation: Distinct length-2 substrings are {ab, ba}, so 2.
mcqChain.hint: Eliminate duplicates from the list.

mcqChain.questionText: For s = "abab", how many distinct substrings of length 3 are there?
mcqChain.explanation: Distinct length-3 substrings are {aba, bab}, so 2. Overlaps are allowed; they are different strings.

wrongExplanations.3: There are only two length-3 windows in a 4-length string.

mcqChain.hint: Count unique strings, overlaps don't matter.

mcqChain.questionText: For s = "abab", how many distinct substrings of length 4 are there?
mcqChain.explanation: Only one substring of length 4 exists: "abab".

wrongExplanations.0: There is exactly one length-4 substring: the whole string.

mcqChain.hint: Length equals n, so there’s only one window.

mcqChain.questionText: For s = "aaaa", what should be the output for lengths 1..4?
mcqChain.diagram: All substrings: a, a, a, a; aa, aa, aa; aaa, aaa; aaaa
mcqChain.explanation: Each length has exactly one distinct string: a, aa, aaa, aaaa.

wrongExplanations.2: That's counting windows, not distinct strings.

mcqChain.hint: Repeated characters collapse duplicates.

mcqChain.questionText: Brute force idea: collect all substrings by length in sets. With n up to 1e5, what are time and memory in the worst case?

options.label: Time ~ O(n log n), Memory ~ O(n)

mcqChain.explanation: There are ~n(n+1)/2 substrings; storing distinct ones across lengths is still quadratic in worst case.

wrongExplanations.0: Storing distinct substrings can also be quadratic.

mcqChain.hint: Consider a string with all distinct characters.

mcqChain.questionText: Fix an end position i. Consider all substrings that end at i. The new distinct substrings introduced at i (not seen earlier) have lengths forming what kind of set?

options.label: An arbitrary scattered set of lengths

mcqChain.explanation: They form a consecutive block (k+1..A], where k is the largest length already seen and A is the maximum possible length at i.

wrongExplanations.0: The boundary is defined by the longest previously seen suffix ending earlier.

mcqChain.hint: There’s a threshold length after which all longer suffixes are new.

mcqChain.questionText: Let A be the maximum length of a substring ending at i, and B be the maximum length of a substring ending at i that also appears earlier (ending before i). How many new distinct substrings are introduced at i?
mcqChain.explanation: New lengths are exactly the integers B+1..A, so count is A - B.
mcqChain.hint: Count integers from B+1 to A.

mcqChain.questionText: We maintain nodes, each with max_len and a fallback link to another node with smaller max_len. If a node has max_len=7 and its link’s max_len=4, which substring lengths does this node represent?
mcqChain.explanation: It represents lengths 5,6,7. That’s (link.max_len, max_len] which equals [5..7].

wrongExplanations.0: Length 4 belongs to the link node, not this one.

mcqChain.hint: Exclude link’s max_len, include this node’s max_len.

mcqChain.questionText: When appending a character c, from the current last node, follow fallback links while adding missing c-transitions to a new node. When should this backward propagation stop?

options.label: As soon as we find a node p with an existing c-transition
Either when p=-1 or when a node with c-transition is found
Always go to the root, even if a c-transition exists earlier

mcqChain.explanation: Stop at the first node with a c-transition or at p=-1. That boundary determines the new node’s fallback.

wrongExplanations.0: You may encounter an existing c-transition earlier.

mcqChain.hint: You stop at the first place where c already leads somewhere.

mcqChain.questionText: If no node along fallbacks had a c-transition (we reached p = -1), what should the fallback of the new node be?
mcqChain.explanation: If nothing matched, fallback goes to root. Root represents empty context.

wrongExplanations.1: We need a valid fallback to maintain propagation.

mcqChain.hint: Fallback must be a valid node index.

mcqChain.questionText: If we stopped at a node p that has a c-transition to q, and max_len[p] + 1 == max_len[q], what should the new node’s fallback be?

options.label: Keep it as initially 0 only if p=0

mcqChain.explanation: In this tight case, the correct fallback is q.

wrongExplanations.2: Root loses necessary context captured by q.

mcqChain.hint: The immediate extension candidate is ideal fallback.

mcqChain.questionText: If we stopped at p with a c-transition to q but max_len[p] + 1 < max_len[q], we split q by creating a clone. What max_len should the clone have?
mcqChain.diagram: Need a split because q is too long
mcqChain.explanation: Clone’s max_len should be exactly max_len[p] + 1 to bridge the gap.

wrongExplanations.1: That would not separate the length boundary correctly.

mcqChain.hint: Bridge to be just one more than p.

mcqChain.questionText: When creating the clone, which transitions and fallback should it initially have?
mcqChain.diagram: Clone of q with adjusted max_len

options.label: Copy all transitions from q; fallback = fallback[q]

mcqChain.explanation: Clone mirrors q’s transitions and fallback; only max_len and incoming edges change.

wrongExplanations.2: Fallback of clone should preserve q’s context.

mcqChain.hint: Clone initially behaves exactly like q.

mcqChain.questionText: After creating the clone, what should be the fallback of q and of the new node?
mcqChain.diagram: q and new point to clone

options.label: Both q and new point to clone
Only q points to clone; new points to q
Only new points to clone; q is unchanged

mcqChain.explanation: Both should point to the clone so length ranges split correctly.

wrongExplanations.2: Leaving q unchanged breaks the range boundary.

mcqChain.hint: Both must share the adjusted boundary node.

mcqChain.questionText: During cloning, which transitions should be redirected to the clone?
mcqChain.diagram: Redirect along fallbacks while edge p --c--> q

options.label: While p != -1 and next[p].get(c) == q, set next[p][c] = clone; then p = fallback[p]
While p != -1 and next[p].get(c) == clone, set next[p][c] = q; then p = fallback[p]
Redirect only at node p where we stopped
Redirect all outgoing edges of p to clone

mcqChain.explanation: Only edges pointing to q via character c along fallbacks should be retargeted to clone.

wrongExplanations.2: Earlier nodes may also point to q via c and must be updated.

mcqChain.hint: Redirect precisely the c-edges to q along the path.

mcqChain.questionText: After building nodes, how do we convert length ranges into per-length counts efficiently?
mcqChain.diagram: For each node: add +1 to (link_len, max_len]

options.label: Use a difference array diff of size n+2: diff[link_len+1] += 1; diff[max_len+1] -= 1, then prefix sum
For each length in the range, increment an array directly
Only increment at max_len, ignoring lower lengths

mcqChain.explanation: Range add via diff array and prefix sum yields counts per length in O(n).

wrongExplanations.1: That is O(range) per node; too slow overall.

mcqChain.hint: Range add trick: +1 at start, -1 after end, then prefix sum.

mcqChain.questionText: Read input and set up main function skeleton.

options.label:
import sys

def main():
    s = sys.stdin.readline().strip()
    n = len(s)

def main():
    import sys
    s = input()
    n = s

import sys

def main():
    s = sys.stdin.read()
    n = int(s)

mcqChain.explanation: We need the line as a string without newline and its length.

wrongExplanations.1: n should be length, not the string itself.
wrongExplanations.2: Input is a string, not an integer.

mcqChain.hint: Use readline().strip() and len().
mcqChain.codeSnippet:
import sys

def main():
    s = sys.stdin.readline().strip()
    n = len(s)

mcqChain.questionText: Initialize arrays for nodes, the root, and the last pointer.

options.label:
nexts = []
    link = []
    length = []
    nexts.append({})
    link.append(-1)
    length.append(0)
    last = 0

nexts = []
    link = []
    length = []
    nexts.append(None)
    link.append(0)
    length.append(-1)
    last = 0

mcqChain.explanation: Root has empty transitions, link=-1, max_len=0, and last starts at root (0).

wrongExplanations.1: Root's link should be -1 and last should be 0.
wrongExplanations.3: Root's length must start at 0 and index 0 must be the root.

mcqChain.hint: Root index is 0; its link is -1.
mcqChain.codeSnippet:
nexts = []
    link = []
    length = []
    nexts.append({})
    link.append(-1)
    length.append(0)
    last = 0

mcqChain.questionText: Start the character extension function: create new state, set its length, and set up to propagate transitions.

options.label:
def sa_extend(c):
        nonlocal last
        cur = len(nexts)
        nexts.append({})
        length.append(length[last] + 1)
        link.append(0)
        p = last

def sa_extend(c):
        cur = len(nexts)
        nexts.append({})
        length.append(length[last] + 1)
        link.append(-1)
        p = last

def sa_extend(c):
        nonlocal last
        cur = len(nexts)
        nexts.append({})
        length.append(length[cur] + 1)
        link.append(0)
        p = last

def sa_extend(c):
        nonlocal last
        cur = len(nexts)
        nexts.append({})
        length.append(length[last])
        link.append(0)
        p = 0

mcqChain.explanation: New state's max_len is last.max_len + 1; initialize link to 0 (may be changed); need nonlocal last; start p from last.

wrongExplanations.1: Missing nonlocal and wrong initial link may be okay but not best; also link often set to 0 then adjusted.
wrongExplanations.3: New state's length must increase by 1 and p should start at last.

mcqChain.hint: Use length[last] + 1 and nonlocal last.
mcqChain.codeSnippet:
def sa_extend(c):
        nonlocal last
        cur = len(nexts)
        nexts.append({})
        length.append(length[last] + 1)
        link.append(0)
        p = last

mcqChain.questionText: Propagate missing transitions and handle the simple fallback cases.
mcqChain.explanation: Add missing c-transitions to cur while walking fallbacks; if none found, fallback is root; else check the tight length condition.

wrongExplanations.1: The loop condition is inverted; also fallback -1 is wrong.
wrongExplanations.3: Not adding transitions loses edges; and fallback -1 is incorrect.

mcqChain.hint: Add edges when c is missing; root fallback is 0.

mcqChain.questionText: Implement the clone case: create clone, redirect edges, and set fallbacks.

options.label:
clone = len(nexts)
                nexts.append(nexts[q].copy())
                length.append(length[p] + 1)
                link.append(link[q])
                while p != -1 and nexts[p].get(c, -1) == q:
                    nexts[p][c] = clone
                    p = link[p]
                link[q] = link[cur] = clone
        last = cur

clone = len(nexts)
                nexts.append({})
                length.append(length[q])
                link.append(0)
                while p != -1 and nexts[p].get(c, -1) == clone:
                    nexts[p][c] = q
                    p = link[p]
                link[q] = cur
        last = cur

clone = len(nexts)
                nexts.append(nexts[q])
                length.append(length[p])
                link.append(link[p])
                while p != -1 and c in nexts[p]:
                    nexts[p][c] = clone
                    p = link[p]
                link[cur] = q
        last = cur

clone = len(nexts)
                nexts.append(nexts[q].copy())
                length.append(length[p] + 1)
                link.append(link[q])
                while p != -1 and nexts[p].get(c, -1) != q:
                    nexts[p][c] = clone
                    p = link[p]
                link[cur] = q
        last = p

mcqChain.explanation: Clone mirrors q’s transitions and fallback, has max_len=p.max_len+1, redirects c-edges that pointed to q, and both q and cur fall back to clone.

wrongExplanations.1: Clone must copy transitions and inherit q’s fallback; redirection condition is wrong.
wrongExplanations.3: Fallbacks and redirection are wrong; last must be cur.

mcqChain.hint: Copy q’s transitions; redirect edges that target q via c.
mcqChain.codeSnippet:
clone = len(nexts)
                nexts.append(nexts[q].copy())
                length.append(length[p] + 1)
                link.append(link[q])
                while p != -1 and nexts[p].get(c, -1) == q:
                    nexts[p][c] = clone
                    p = link[p]
                link[q] = link[cur] = clone
        last = cur

mcqChain.questionText: Loop over characters to build the structure.
mcqChain.explanation: Extend with each character in order.

wrongExplanations.3: Passing the whole string each time is wrong.

mcqChain.hint: Iterate characters directly.

mcqChain.questionText: Convert node ranges to counts using a difference array and print the result.

options.label:
diff = [0] * (n + 2)
    for v in range(1, len(nexts)):
        l = length[link[v]] + 1
        r = length[v]
        if l <= r:
            diff[l] += 1
            diff[r + 1] -= 1
    res = []
    cur = 0
    for i in range(1, n + 1):
        cur += diff[i]
        res.append(str(cur))
    sys.stdout.write(" ".join(res))

diff = [0] * (n + 1)
    for v in range(len(nexts)):
        l = length[link[v]]
        r = length[v]
        diff[l] += 1
        diff[r] -= 1
    res = []
    cur = 0
    for i in range(n):
        cur += diff[i]
        res.append(str(cur))
    print(" ".join(res))

diff = [0] * (n + 2)
    for v in range(1, len(nexts)):
        l = length[link[v]]
        r = length[v]
        diff[l] += 1
        diff[r + 1] -= 1
    res = []
    cur = 0
    for i in range(1, n + 1):
        cur += diff[i]
        res.append(str(cur))
    print(" ", res)

mcqChain.explanation: Use diff at l=link_len+1 and at r+1; prefix sum yields counts for 1..n. Skip v=0 (root).

wrongExplanations.1: Off-by-one errors in l and array sizes.
wrongExplanations.2: Nested loops are too slow for n=1e5.

mcqChain.hint: Remember to add at l and subtract at r+1.
mcqChain.codeSnippet:
diff = [0] * (n + 2)
    for v in range(1, len(nexts)):
        l = length[link[v]] + 1
        r = length[v]
        if l <= r:
            diff[l] += 1
            diff[r + 1] -= 1
    res = []
    cur = 0
    for i in range(1, n + 1):
        cur += diff[i]
        res.append(str(cur))
    sys.stdout.write(" ".join(res))

mcqChain.questionText: Add the standard Python entry point after main().
mcqChain.explanation: Standard Python idiom to call main.
mcqChain.hint: Double underscores on both sides.

mcqChain.questionText: Run your reasoning on s = "abab". What should the program output?
mcqChain.explanation: Matches the example: 2 2 2 1.

wrongExplanations.1: Length-2 distinct substrings are only {ab, ba}.

mcqChain.hint: List per length and deduplicate mentally.

mcqChain.questionText: For s = "abca", what should the output be?
mcqChain.explanation: Distinct per length are 3,3,2,1 respectively.

wrongExplanations.3: There are only 3 distinct length-1 substrings.

mcqChain.hint: Enumerate short lengths quickly.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the time complexity of building this structure and producing the counts (for alphabet a–z)?
mcqChain.explanation: Each character is processed with amortized constant work; counting with the difference array is linear.

wrongExplanations.1: No sorting or logarithmic factors are used.

mcqChain.hint: Each step adds at most two nodes and redirects along fallbacks.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the space complexity of this approach in terms of n?
mcqChain.explanation: The structure has at most about 2n nodes and a linear number of transitions overall.

wrongExplanations.2: We never store all substrings explicitly.

mcqChain.hint: Think in terms of nodes and total transitions.

mcqChain.questionText: Why is the difference array sized as n+2 instead of n+1?

options.label: We write at index r+1 when r = n, so we need an extra slot
To avoid Python index errors unrelated to logic
Because we also count length 0 substrings
It is arbitrary; any size works

mcqChain.explanation: We do diff[r+1] -= 1. When r = n, we need index n+1.
mcqChain.hint: Check the largest r and the r+1 write.

finalCodeSnippets:
import sys

def main():
    s = sys.stdin.readline().strip()
    n = len(s)

nexts = []
    link = []
    length = []
    nexts.append({})
    link.append(-1)
    length.append(0)
    last = 0

def sa_extend(c):
        nonlocal last
        cur = len(nexts)
        nexts.append({})
        length.append(length[last] + 1)
        link.append(0)
        p = last

clone = len(nexts)
                nexts.append(nexts[q].copy())
                length.append(length[p] + 1)
                link.append(link[q])
                while p != -1 and nexts[p].get(c, -1) == q:
                    nexts[p][c] = clone
                    p = link[p]
                link[q] = link[cur] = clone
        last = cur

diff = [0] * (n + 2)
    for v in range(1, len(nexts)):
        l = length[link[v]] + 1
        r = length[v]
        if l <= r:
            diff[l] += 1
            diff[r + 1] -= 1
    res = []
    cur = 0
    for i in range(1, n + 1):
        cur += diff[i]
        res.append(str(cur))
    sys.stdout.write(" ".join(res))

reasoningChain.question: Why is brute force (enumerating all substrings) infeasible for n up to 1e5?
reasoningChain.insight: There are O(n^2) substrings; storing/checking them is too slow and memory-heavy.

reasoningChain.question: What do new distinct substrings look like at each end position i?
reasoningChain.insight: They form a consecutive block of lengths (B, A], so we can account for ranges instead of individual substrings.

reasoningChain.question: How can we process the string online to know A and B efficiently?
reasoningChain.insight: Maintain a graph of nodes with transitions and fallbacks that captures all substrings ending at positions; each extension yields the count A−B.

reasoningChain.question: How are node length ranges defined?
reasoningChain.insight: Each node represents lengths (link.max_len, max_len]; this partitions substrings by their maximal lengths.

reasoningChain.question: How to update the structure when appending a character?
reasoningChain.insight: Add a new node, propagate missing transitions, and if an existing transition jumps too far, split by cloning to maintain correct length boundaries.

reasoningChain.question: How to compute the per-length distribution from nodes?
reasoningChain.insight: For each node v≠root, add +1 to the range [link_len+1, max_len] using a difference array and then take a prefix sum.
